questions.question: What does the research show about competitive recreation and mental health outcomes?
options: Competition is always harmful to mental health and should be avoided
Competition is universally beneficial for everyone's mental health
Outcomes depend on competitive culture and individual motivation — supportive mastery-focused environments enhance wellbeing; toxic performance-obsessed cultures cause harm
Only professional athletes benefit from competitive activities
questions.explanation: The evidence (MODERATE overall) shows that competitive recreation's mental health effects depend critically on TWO factors: (1) The competitive culture (supportive mastery-focused vs. toxic win-at-all-costs), and (2) Individual motivation (intrinsic/mastery vs. external validation-seeking). Healthy competition in supportive cultures enhances wellbeing; unhealthy competition in toxic cultures causes harm. Competition isn't inherently good or bad.

questions.question: Which of the following are characteristics of HEALTHY competitive engagement? (Select all that apply)
options: Mastery goals focused on self-improvement, not just defeating others
Intrinsic motivation — enjoying the challenge itself
Self-worth is tied to winning and losing outcomes
Growth mindset — losses are learning opportunities
Can genuinely celebrate opponents' success without feeling threatened
Winning at any cost, including bending rules or violating values
questions.explanation: Healthy competitive engagement includes: mastery goals (self-improvement focus), intrinsic motivation (enjoying challenge), growth mindset (learning from setbacks), and ability to celebrate others' success. UNHEALTHY patterns include: self-worth contingent on outcomes, and win-at-any-cost mentality. The key distinction is mastery-orientation vs. performance-obsession.

questions.question: According to Self-Determination Theory, which type of motivation is healthiest for competitive recreation?
options: External regulation — competing for rewards, status, or recognition
Introjected regulation — competing because you'd feel guilty or ashamed if you didn't
Intrinsic motivation — competing because you genuinely enjoy the challenge and the activity itself
Amotivation — not knowing why you compete anymore
questions.explanation: Intrinsic motivation — where you compete because you genuinely enjoy the challenge, experience flow, and find the activity inherently satisfying — is the healthiest form for wellbeing and sustained engagement. External regulation (status-seeking) and introjected regulation (guilt-driven) are less autonomous and more harmful. Intrinsic motivation supports wellbeing even when you lose, because enjoyment comes from the activity itself, not just outcomes.

questions.question: Which competitive league shows the most RED FLAGS for toxic culture that would likely harm wellbeing?
options: A league with skill tiers, inclusive messaging, post-game socializing, emphasis on effort and learning, and fair play enforcement
A league dominated by elite players with no skill tiers, win-focused messaging, coaches yelling at mistakes, rule-bending tolerated, and no social interaction
A social-first league emphasizing fun and community, with mixed skill levels and supportive atmosphere
A league with moderate competition, clear progression pathways, and balanced focus on skill development and performance
questions.explanation: League B shows multiple red flags for toxic culture: no skill matching (beginners playing elite competitors), win-at-all-costs messaging, yelling/blaming culture, rule-bending tolerated, no social connection, and exclusivity. This environment creates ego-threat, anxiety, and conditional self-worth. League A shows healthy mastery culture; League C shows healthy social-recreational culture. Culture assessment is critical before committing to competitive environments.

questions.question: When is competitive recreation particularly risky and should likely be avoided or postponed?
options: When you're a beginner and haven't mastered the activity yet
When your self-worth is fragile and already tied to performance/achievement outcomes
When you have limited time in your schedule
When you prefer solo activities over team activities
questions.explanation: Competitive recreation is particularly risky when self-worth is already fragile or performance-contingent. Competition will exacerbate this pattern: wins temporarily boost fragile self-worth, losses devastate it, creating anxiety and shame spirals. Other high-risk situations: severe perfectionism, mental health crisis, and no healthy competitive environments available. Being a beginner, having limited time, or preferring solo activities are fit considerations, not contraindications.
